key_insight: By constraining networks to 3D representations, the paper shows how architecture and activation choices change low-dimensional topology, with ResNets and transformers most able to alter linking number and nonmonotonic feedforward nets matching them.
engineering_relevance: Topological invariants like linking number can serve as a design lens for comparing neural architectures and understanding which components increase expressivity without relying on width.
method: Study feedforward nets, ResNets, and transformers with layer width fixed to d = 3 (R^3).
Track changes in low-dimensional topological invariants, focusing on linking number.
Compare expressivity across monotonic, nonmonotonic, invertible/flow-based, residual, and attention-based models; extend results to d > 3.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports full proofs plus empirical experiments supporting the expressivity ordering across architectures and activation types.
watch_item: The conclusions are derived in a highly constrained low-dimensional setting (especially d = 3), so direct translation to practical high-dimensional training may be limited.
paper_title: Low-dimensional topology of deep neural networks
